perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information” means collectively and individually all or any information of whatsoever nature, di sclosed in any form, whether oral, visual, written or in electronic form (including, without limitation, any documentation, reports, calculations, processes, methodologies, manuals and membership data ) relating to [Company name] and FORS operators, audit processes and commercial data together with all records or copies or e xtracts thereof in whatever media held.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” means any data deemed confidential by the disclosing party, regardless of its nature or form, including information already in the public domain, previously known by the receiving party, or disclosed without restriction by a third party.
perturbation.explanation: Redefining 'Confidential Information' to include data already in the public domain, previously known, or disclosed without restriction contradicts standard legal interpretations of confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secrets Law and Common Law Definition of Confidentiality
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act defines a trade secret as information that is not generally known or readily ascertainable. The modified text includes information already in the public domain which is contradictory.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Uniform act in the United States Intellectual property Authors' rights Copyleft Copyright Database right Farmers' rights Geographical indication Indigenous intellectual property Industrial design right Integrated circuit layout design protection Intellectual property infringement Literary property Moral rights Patent Peasants' rights Plant breeders' rights Plant genetic resources Proprietary software Related rights Supplementary protection certificate Trade dress Trade secret Trademark Utility model Patent infringement Related topics Abandonware Artificial intelligence and copyright Brand protection Copyright abolition Copyright troll Criticism of copyright Bioprospecting Biopiracy Idea–expression distinction Limitations and exceptions to copyright Fair dealing Fair use Paraphrasing Right to quote Orphan work Patent troll Pirate Party Public domain Outline of intellectual property Outline of patents Higher categories: Property and Property law v t e The Uniform Trade Secrets Act ( UTSA ), published by the Uniform Law Commission (ULC) in 1979 and amended in 1985, is a model law designed for adoption by U.S. states . [ 1 ] It was developed to resolve inconsistencies in the treatment of trade secrets across different states. [ 1 ] (p1) The UTSA provides unitary definitions for "trade secret" and "trade secret misappropriation" and establishes a single statute of limitations, replacing the varied property, quasi-contractual, and fiduciary liability frameworks previously applied under common law . [ 1 ] (p2) To date, 49 states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands have adopted the UTSA, with New York as the remaining exceptions. [ 2 ] Motivation [ edit ] A prefatory note to the UTSA states some original motivations for the act: A valid patent provides a legal monopoly for seventeen years in exchange for public disclosure of an invention. If, however, the courts ultimately decide that the Patent Office improperly issued a patent, an invention has been disclosed to competitors with no corresponding benefit. In view of the substantial number of patents that the courts invalidate, many businesses now elect to protect commercially valuable information by relying on the state trade secret protection law. — Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Prefatory Note [ 3 ] The UTSA made note of the commercial value and competitive advantages inherent in trade secrets. Unlike patent protection, which was addressed at the federal level, trade secret misappropriation was addressed at the state level. [ 3 ] In the United States there existed a prevalence of interstate commercial transactions that extended beyond the jurisdiction of individual state legislation. For example, goods may have been manufactured in State A, warehoused in State B, sold from State C, and delivered in State D. As a result, the UTSA sought to alleviate the uneven development and "uncertainty concerning the parameters of trade secret protection" by recommending a uniform trade secret law and, at the same time, allowing the states the flexibility to meet local circumstances by modifying the text as enacted in eac
perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines 'Confidential Information' to include data already in the public domain, previously known, or disclosed without restriction. This contradicts the standard legal interpretation of confidentiality, particularly in the context of trade secrets. The Law Explanation correctly points out that the Uniform Trade Secrets Act defines a trade secret as information that is not generally known or readily ascertainable. The provided snippet from Wikipedia regarding the Uniform Trade Secrets Act supports this interpretation, stating that the UTSA provides definitions for 'trade secret'. By including publicly available information as confidential, the changed text directly contradicts the established legal definition of a trade secret, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: to keep the Confidential Information secret and acknowledges the proprietary nature of the Confidential Information;
perturbation.changed_text: to maintain the Confidential Information; the proprietary nature of the Confidential Information is not acknowledged;
perturbation.explanation: Removing the acknowledgment of the 'proprietary nature' weakens the protection afforded to the disclosing party's confidential information and could imply that there is no ownership attached to the information being shared, against established common law principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Principles of Trade Secret Protection
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (First) of Torts § 757 discusses the protection of trade secrets, implying a proprietary interest. By stating the proprietary nature is 'not acknowledged', the contract weakens the protection normally afforded under common law.

perturbation.justification: The original text acknowledges the proprietary nature of confidential information, while the changed text explicitly denies this acknowledgment. The Law Explanation supports that the Restatement (First) of Torts § 757 implies a proprietary interest in trade secrets. By removing this acknowledgment, the modified contract weakens the protection afforded under common law, potentially making the information less defensible as a trade secret or proprietary information. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, the LLM explanation and Snippet 2 support the contradiction, even though Snippet 2 is only a PDF file.

perturbation.original_text: The above duties of confidentiality shall not apply to information that the receiving Party can demonstrate by reasonable documentary proof: - 2.2.1 to hav e been in the public domain at the time of receipt or to subsequently entered the public domain through no fault of the receiving Party;
perturbation.changed_text: The above duties of confidentiality shall not apply to information that the receiving Party alleges to have been in the public domain. The alleging, without proof, shall be sufficient to consider the information to be in the public domain.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'reasonable documentary proof' requirement for demonstrating information is in the public domain weakens the obligation on the receiving party and contradicts the need for evidence in legal proceedings. Removing the evidentiary requirement is a violation of standard contract interpretation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Rules of Evidence and Contract Interpretation Principles
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rules of Evidence require admissible evidence to be presented. By stating that mere allegation is sufficient, the contract contradicts these evidence rules.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 402. General Admissibility of Relevant Evidence | Federal Rules of Evidence | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 402. General Admissibility of Relevant Evidence Relevant evidence is admissible unless any of the following provides otherwise: the United States Constitution; a federal statute; these rules; or other rules prescribed by the Supreme Court. Irrelevant evidence is not admissible. Notes (Pub. L. 93–595, §1, Jan. 2, 1975, 88 Stat. 1931; Apr. 26, 2011, eff. Dec. 1, 2011.) Notes of Advisory Committee on Proposed Rules The provisions that all relevant evidence is admissible, with certain exceptions, and that evidence which is not relevant is not admissible are “a presupposition involved in the very conception of a rational system of evidence.” Thayer, Preliminary Treatise on Evidence 264 (1898). They constitute the foundation upon which the structure of admission and exclusion rests. For similar provisions see California Evidence Code §§350, 351. Provisions that all relevant evidence is admissible are found in Uniform Rule 7(f); Kansas Code of Civil Procedure §60–407(f); and New Jersey Evidence Rule 7(f); but the exclusion of evidence which is not relevant is left to implication. Not all relevant evidence is admissible. The exclusion of relevant evidence occurs in a variety of situations and may be called for by these rules, by the Rules of Civil and Criminal Procedure, by Bankruptcy Rules, by Act of Congress, or by constitutional considerations. Succeeding rules in the present article, in response to the demands of particular policies, require the exclusion of evidence despite its relevancy. In addition, Article V recognizes a number of privileges; Article VI imposes limitations upon witnesses and the manner of dealing with them; Article VII specifies requirements with respect to opinions and expert testimony; Article VIII excludes hearsay not falling within an exception; Article IX spells out the handli
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires 'reasonable documentary proof' to demonstrate information is in the public domain. The changed text removes this requirement, stating that a mere allegation is sufficient. This contradicts the fundamental legal principle that evidence is required to support claims, as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states 'Relevant evidence is admissible unless any of the following provides otherwise...'. By allowing allegations without proof, the contract undermines the need for evidence and weakens the standard for demonstrating information is in the public domain, which creates a legal contradiction, particularly as the Law Explanation states that Federal Rules of Evidence require admissible evidence.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall indemnify and hol d harmless the other Party against all loss, damage and expense (including legal expenses) whatsoever and howsoever arising out of or in connection with any breach by the first Party or its employees of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall indemnify and hold harmless the other Party against all loss, damage and expense (excluding legal expenses) whatsoever and howsoever arising out of or in connection with any breach by the first Party or its employees of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Excluding legal expenses from the indemnification clause reduces the protection afforded to the non-breaching party and contradicts the common understanding of full indemnification.
perturbation.law_citation: Black's Law Dictionary Definition of Indemnification
perturbation.law_explanation: Indemnification generally includes all losses and expenses arising from a breach, including legal fees. By excluding legal expenses, the modified text reduces the scope of indemnification typically understood and expected.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: indemnify | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you indemnify To indemnify, also known as indemnity or indemnification, means compensating a person for damages or losses they have incurred or will incur related to a specified accident, incident, or event. Typically, parties make a written agreement in which one party (indemnitor) promises to indemnify the other party (indemnitee) for future specified losses. The more common form of an indemnification agreement is the insurance policy. This often happens when an insurance company, as part of an individual's insurance policy, agrees to indemnify the insured person for losses that the insured person incurred due to accident or property damage. In this sort of agreement, party A would hire insurance company B. If party A and party C are involved in some incident that results in property or personal damage (ex: a car accident), insurance company B would assume any liability for which Party A might be responsible. After that, insurance company B would typically sue party C to recover damages. See Hercules Inc. et al v. United States, 516 U.S. 417 (1996). [Last reviewed in March of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords contract contract law liability Wex accidents and injuries COMMERCE commercial activities insurance PROPERTY contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the principle of indemnification by excluding legal expenses. The original text includes 'all loss, damage and expense (including legal expenses),' providing comprehensive coverage. The change removes 'legal expenses,' directly reducing the scope of indemnification. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'indemnify' as 'compensating a person for damages or losses they have incurred or will incur related to a specified accident, incident, or event.' Excluding legal expenses limits the compensation, conflicting with the standard understanding of indemnification as covering all losses, including legal costs.
